entries.title: About the Course
entries.content:
This course includes the following topic(s): Old Testament: Grades 7-12, New Testament: Grade 9, Church History: Grades 7-9, Bible History: Grade 9

About Old Testament: Grades 7-12
From 7th grade through high school, students read through the OT chronologically. For 2026-2027 they read 2 Samuel-I Kings, 1 & 2 Chronicles, Psalms and Proverbs (selections) and Ecclesiastes. 12th grade will also read Song of Solomon. With the aid of study Bible notes and selections from study helps such as the Old Testament Today or The Bible Project, this course allows students to meditate on Scripture and engage with the historical and cultural context of the Bible when it was written, as well as the way the OT continues to be relevant as God's word today.

About Church History: Grades 7-9
Students read about important events and influential people from Church History using biographies and narrative-driven overviews of the time period. In this course, students delve into the 19th century and learn the stories of a variety of Christians.

About New Testament: Grade 9
Each year in High School, students read one of the gospels along with several other New Testament epistles. For 2026-2027, they read the gospel of Luke along with Galatians, Philippians, and Colossians. Using their study Bibles and other resources, students encounter scripture and seek to understand the cultural, historical, and theological context of their readings in order to more deeply appreciate the richness of the Bible and the humility needed in order to live out God's word today.

About Bible History: Grade 9
This course uses the book God's Big Picture to give students a zoomed-out view of Bible history and show the outline of God's Kingdom plan from beginning to end.

entries.title: Placement & Combining Tips
entries.content:
Old Testament: Grades 7-12
Students in Grades 7-12 read the same passages of Scripture, but the lesson plans include a variety of discussion questions and extra study helps differentiated by grade to help students engage at various depths. This makes these lessons flexible for individual students and/or a multi-age context. Younger students in Grades 5-6 can be combined with older students if they have a good general understanding of the narratives and overarching story of the OT.

Church History: Grades 7-9
Can be combined with Grades 5-6.

New Testament: Grade 9
Appropriate for any HS grades. Students new to reading the Bible for themselves or unused to commentaries or study notes may find the lessons in Grades 7-8 a gentler introduction to these study practices.

Bible History: Grade 9
Appropriate for any HS grades.

blocks.text: Take a moment to recall what you read previously in the same book or topic.

Read the passage assigned or read for the number of minutes allocated. If a book is challenging for you, try stopping to narrate to yourself silently now and then to make sure you are tracking. Or you could listen to an audiobook version of the text and read along. You may also want to read aloud to yourself. If a book is an easy read, enjoy the ride! Keep an eye out for quotes to put in your Commonplace Book or events or characters to put down in your Book of Centuries.

Process and integrate your reading by narrating either orally or in writing. Some lesson plans will give you a suggestion of how best to narrate. You might want to set a timer to keep yourself on track, so you stop reading with enough time to narrate.

Read notes in your Study Bible or commentary. Look up places mentioned in the text in your Bible atlas. Add something to your Book of Centuries or Commonplace Book. Jot down additional reflections and thoughts in your Bible notebook or share with others.

Read over the lesson plans to familiarize yourself with the content for accountability and interest. If doing the lessons together, use a picture, a map, or an engaging question to rouse students' interest before reading. You may also highlight one or two unfamiliar terms if needed. If in a classroom context, you may choose to leave larger doctrinal conversations for students to have with their parents at home.

lessons.title: 30m Old Testament: Grades 7-12 - Lesson 1
lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: Old Testament Today, notebook
➜ INTRO (For Grades 7-12)
This year you will be reading 2 Samuel, I Kings, I & 2 Chronicles, selections of Psalms, Proverbs and Ecclesiastes. You will be reading the passages chronologically or in the order of the events. "The goal of studying the Bible chronologically is not to replace the time-honored canonical arrangement, but instead to honor time as a setting in which the biblical record appeared. Readers who study the Bible this way will return to their traditional Bibles better equipped to read them. Acquiring a sense of the flow of the Bible's story will put flesh and blood on its message" (from Chronological Guide to the Bible).
1. Your ESV Concise Study Bible (Grades 7-8) or Cultural Backgrounds 
Study Bible (Grade 9-12) will have maps, articles, and notes to help you
along the way. If you have a question about a specific verse, see if there is
a note about that verse below the text.
2. Use your Student Bible Atlas or atlas in your Bible to locate places as
needed.
3. We will also utilize Old Testament Today and the Bible Project website 
as a handbook for helping us understand the background information we 
need in order to be informed readers.

➜ VIEW
1. Throughout the year, you will watch short videos of Dr. John Walton, the
co-author of OT Today. The videos were created specifically for Alveary
students. We hope they help you understand the Bible better. 
2. You will be reading selections from the Psalms throughout the year. Watch this video to learn more about the Psalms. 
∞ Video Link: Psalms

➜ READ, NARRATE & RESPOND
The psalms are difficult to tie to a specific historical context. They are the worship songs of Israel and are intended to apply to the needs of all worshippers across time. Some psalms do have historical superscriptions that make it possible to associate them with specific events, but these make up only about 10% of all the psalms. Another criterion is to place the psalms ascribed to David (or Solomon or Moses) during the life of that person. A second criterion is to place pslams according to their type (from Chronological Guide to the Bible)>
Psalms 79-82

lessons.title: 30m New Testament: Grade 9 - Lesson 1
lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: Cultural Backgrounds Study Bible, 

➜ INTRO
Each year in HS, we will be reading one of the Gospels along with our study of the rest of the New Testament books. This year, we will be reading Luke along with Galatians, Philippians, and Colossians. It is helpful to be continually reminded of who Jesus is and how he interacted with people. The Gospel accounts were designed to be read many times. There will be insights gained that you would only pick up after multiple readings and by paying attention to repeated words and themes. 

We will make use of the Cultural Backgrounds Study Bible notes and maps, and some Bible Project videos/articles/podcasts to help us understand what Luke and Paul are saying to their audiences. Then we can think about what new thoughts about God we gain in the process. You do not need to read all the study notes for all the verses. Pick and choose based on which verses you have questions about. Use a digital or physical notebook to record your thoughts as you work your way through the readings. 

➜ VIEW & NARRATE
Today, we will set the stage for reading Mark by gaining some context. Narrate silently in your head or by talking to yourself to go over the main points. Or, jot them down in your journal.
∞ Video Link: How to Read the New Testament
∞ Video Link: How to Read the Gospel
∞ Video Link: Mark Summary
 
➜ PRINT
You may want to print out the summary of the book to reference as you read.
∞ Image Link: Mark

➜ MAPWORK
When places are named in the text, refer to a map in the study Bible. Finding the places on the map is very helpful for seeing the stories in the context of the land. Sketching your own maps of journeys or regions can be helpful too.

lessons.title: 30m Church History: Grades 7-9 - Lesson 1
lessons.body:
William Wilberforce
⍞ Materials: Renewal: The Church that Expands Outward

➜ INTRO
1. This year, we are learning about people who lived during the 19th century (1800-1900). We will read how these Christians lived out Jesus' commands. When Jesus was asked what the greatest command was, he replied, "You shall love the Lord your God with all your heart and with all your soul and with all your mind. This is the great and first commandment. And a second is like it: You shall love your neighbor as yourself." (Matthew 22:37-39)
2. Today, you will read about an Englishman named William Wilberforce (1759-1833) who was a member of Parliament, the lawmaking group in the government of Great Britain. Parliament consisted of two houses: the House of Lords and the House of Commons. The English had been part of the slave trade since 1662. In 1770, out of a total of one hundred thousand slaves a year from West Africa, British ships transported more than half. English considered the slave trade inseparably linked with the commerce and national security of Great Britain.

➜ VIEW
∞ Image Link: Painting of William Wilberforce

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.93-100 "The striking of" - "after this event."

• Wilberforce had considered withdrawing from public life for the sake of his faith. However, his pastor John Newton advised him, "It is hoped and believed that the Lord has raised you up for the good of his church and for the good of the nation." What do you think about that advice?

lessons.body:
Emilie Mallet
⍞ Materials: Renewal: The Church that Expands Outward

➜ RECAP
What do you remember about our last reading?

➜ INTRO
Today, you will read about a French lady from the 1800s who may be unknown to you but has an interesting story of love and service to those in need. She could have just enjoyed her upper-class lifestyle but wanted to show the love of Christ to others. 

➜ VIEW
1. ∞ Image Link: Emilie Mallet
2. The Pantheon in Paris (not to be confused with the Pantheon in Rome) is mentioned in this chapter. It is a mausoleum or monument that houses the remains of distinguished French citizens, 
∞ Image Link: Pantheon in Paris

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
1. p.109-117 "The shrieks could" - "margins of society."
2. Read these excerpts from the biography A Christian woman; being the life of Mme. Jules Mallet, translated by Mrs. H.N. Goodhart. Most of them are journal entries from Emilie about the circumstances in Paris. 
∞ PDF Link: Excerpts from A Christian Woman

• What did you learn about Emilie from her journal entries?

➜ VIEW
Have you seen Toile de Jouy fabric before? It is actually French for "cloth from Jouy." It was produced by Emilie's father and can still be found today on decorative fabrics and wallpaper. 
∞ Image Link: Toile de Jouy

lessons.title: 30m Church History: Grades 7-9 - Lesson 4
lessons.body:
Sojourner Truth
⍞ Materials: Renewal: The Church that Expands Outward

Note: We are skipping the chapter on Charles Spurgeon since we are reading a whole book about him starting in Term 2.

➜ RECAP
Who did you read about last week?

➜ INTRO
Isabella Baumfree was born a slave to a Dutch master in New York c.1797. At age 46 or 47, she had a vision of God telling her to “travel up an’ down the land showin’ the people their sins an’ bein’ a sign unto them.” This she did, with a new name that defined her mission—Sojourner Truth. She never learned to read or write but boldly spread a message of freedom for Blacks and women, as you will read about in this chapter. 

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
1. p.127-133 "The crowd outside" - "that historic building."
2. Read the excerpt "Her Escape" from her memoir p.41.
∞ Link: The Narrative of Sojourner Truth

➜ VIEW
The bronze bust of abolitionist and women's rights advocate Sojourner Truth is the first sculpture to honor an African American woman in the United States Capitol.
∞ Image Link: Bronze bust of Sojourner Truth
lessons.teacherNotes:
• IMPORTANT DATES
1851 - Sojourner Truth addresses the Ohio Women's Rights Convention

lessons.title: 30m Church History: Grades 7-9 - Lesson 5
lessons.body:
Robert Moffat
⍞ Materials: Renewal: The Church that Expands Outward

➜ INTRO
"Robert Moffat is sometimes regarded as the patriarch of South African missions, a man who had a significant influence in that region for more than half a century. Yet even during his own lifetime, he was overshadowed by his famous son-in-law, often being referred to as 'the father-in-law of David Livingstone.' Moffat, nevertheless, was the far greater missionary of the two. He was an evangelist, a translator, an educator, a diplomat, and an explorer, and he effectively combined those roles to become one of the truly great missionaries to Africa." (Ruth Tucker, From Jerusalem to Irian Jaya) 

➜ VIEW
∞ Image Link: Picture of Robert Moffat

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
1. p.135-141 "The setting sun" - "of Jesus Christ."
2. This short excerpt from the biography Robert Moffat: Pioneer in Africa by Cecil Northcott talks about the difficulties and joys of translating the Bible into the Tswana (also known as Setswana) language.  
∞ PDF Link: Excerpt from Moffat biography

lessons.title: 30m Church History: Grades 7-9 - Lesson 6
lessons.body:
Hudson Taylor
⍞ Materials: Renewal: The Church that Expands Outward

➜ INTRO
"Hudson Taylor was born in Yorkshire, England, in 1832. His father was a pharmacist as well as a Methodist lay preacher who instilled in his son a passion for missions. Before he had reached his fifth birthday, the child was telling visitors that he wanted to some day be a missionary, and China (through the influence of his father) was the land that intrigued him the most." "His sights were set on reaching the whole of China, all 400 million people, and to that end he labored, though not single-handedly. He had a knack for organization, and he possessed a magnetic personality that drew men and women to him and to his point of view. The China Inland Mission was his creation and was a model for future faith missions. In his own lifetime the missionary force under him grew to more than eight hundred, and in the decades following his death it continued to expand." (Ruth Tucker, From Jerusalem to Irian Jaya)

➜ VIEW
∞ Image Link: Picture of Maria and Hudson Taylor

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
1. p.143-150 "The gathering room" - "passing in 1905."
2. ∞ PDF Link: Excerpt from Hudson Taylor Biography p.98-101 (end at "of Chinese dress.")
This excerpt tells about the first time Hudson Taylor changed his appearance to fit into the Chinese culture. It comes from the biography J. Hudson Taylor: God's Man in China by his son and daughter-in-law. The excerpt will mention his queue, which is a braid of hair usually worn hanging at the back of the head. It begins in the middle of a chapter when Hudson is traveling with a fellow missionary Dr. Parker, and they are looking for a place to set up a mission base. Dr. Parker gets called to minister and work at a hospital in another village, but Hudson is left alone to figure out where to go next and to find a place to rent. 

• Hudson Taylor mirrored the method and motive of the Apostle Paul, who testified in 1 Corinthians 9:20: “To the Jews I became like a Jew, to win the Jews. To those under the law I became like one under the law (though I myself am not under the law), so as to win those under the law..." What do you think about his method of dressing and looking like a Chinese person in order to reach them for the gospel? What would be the benefits, and what might be some objections?

• IMPORTANT DATES

⍞ Materials: Renewal: The Church that Expands Outward

➜ INTRO
"Except for two twentieth-century evangelists, Billy Sunday and Billy Graham, no American has had the privilege of personally presenting the gospel of Jesus Christ to so many people. Indeed, by the time of D.L. Moody’s death in 1899, the secretary of the International Committee of the Young Men’s Christian Association (YMCA) declared that 'Mr. Moody delivered the gospel message in a larger number of places, to a larger number of persons, a larger number of times than any man who ever lived.'" (Lyle Dorsett, A Passion for Souls) Perhaps you have never heard of D.L. Moody, but he has impacted many lives, and his impact continues through the college Moody Bible Institute in Chicago, Illinois.

lessons.title: 30m Church History: Grades 7-9 - Lesson 10
lessons.body:
Bible Translation
⍞ Materials: Links

➜ INTRO
If the Bible had never been translated into English, would you be able to read and understand God's message of salvation? We read about many different missionaries who translated the Bible into the native language of the people. Translation work continues to this day. Here are the latest statistics from Wycliffe Bible Translators (wycliffe.org.uk/statistics/): 1 in 5 people are still waiting for the Bible in their language, 7,393 languages in the world, 3,248 languages have no Scripture—yet (down 20 from last month).

Today you will watch videos about Bible translation and how technology is speeding up the translation process in unprecedented ways. 

➜ VIEW
∞ Video Link: Translating for Understanding
∞ Video Link: Church-Led Bible Translation in the Pacific
∞ Video Link: Solving the Technical Challenges

(Optional) You can explore the Wycliffe Translators website to learn more about Bible translation in different regions.
∞ Link: Global Overview

• What did you learn about Bible translation that you didn't know before? 
• How has technology helped the process?
• What questions do you still have about it?

links.text: Church-Led Bible Translation in the Pacific

lessons.body:
The Metropolitan Tabernacle
⍞ Materials: Spurgeon: A Biography, Book of Centuries

➜ RECAP 
What do you recall about your reading about Spurgeon from last time? 

➜ INTRO
The Metropolitan Tabernacle would be the church where Spurgeon would spend the rest of his days as a preacher. In today's reading, he will also speak to a record-setting crowd of 23,654 at The Crystal Palace, the place he and Susannah had spent so much time. Imagine speaking - with just your voice! - to that large a crowd! 

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Ch.9 p.91-99 "Spurgeon had been" - "called him home."

➜ VIEW
∞ Image Link: Metropolitan Tabernacle
The link shows the Metropolitan Tabernacle in more recent times. The Tabernacle is still active as a church to this day, although it has had to be rebuilt twice, once due to a fire and then after a bombing during World War II. It has a slightly different design to its front after its latest rebuild.

lessons.body:
Books
⍞ Materials: Spurgeon: A Biography

➜ RECAP 
What do you recall about your reading about Spurgeon from last time? 

➜ INTRO
In today's reading, Spurgeon's library of 12,000 books is mentioned. Spurgeon felt it was imperative that those who were teaching the Bible be learned and readers of books. In fact, he gave two pieces of advice about books to students: 1. Buy a few, high-quality books; 2. “Master those books you have.” “Read them thoroughly,” he wrote. “Bathe in them until they saturate you. Read and re-read them, masticate them, and digest them. Let them go into your very self. Peruse a good book several times, and make notes and analyses of it. A student will find that his mental constitution is more affected by one book thoroughly mastered than by twenty books which he has merely skimmed, lapping at them, as the classic proverb puts it ‘As the dogs drink of Nilus.’”

He is also quoted as having said, “Visit many good books, but live in the Bible.” As time allows, view the extra helping link to see a tour of a library that now holds half of his collection of books, including a commentary on Psalms from 1476. 

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Ch.16 p.163-175 "Between 1875 and" - "of the work."

lessons.title: 30m Church History: Grades 7-9 - Lesson 32
lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: An Honorable Heritage: The Pandita Ramabai Story in Her Own Words

➜ INTRO
This is the story of Pandita Ramabai who was born in India in 1858. She tells the story of her childhood, her hardships, and coming to faith in Christ. She also translated the Bible into Marathi so the uneducated women of lower castes could read the Bible. She has a remarkable story of how the light of the gospel found her in the darkest places. 

➜ VIEW
∞ Image Link: Photo of Pandita and her daughter

➜ BACKGROUND
India's caste system divides Hindus into four main categories—Brahmins, Kshatriyas, Vaishyas, and the Shudras. Many believe that the groups originated from Brahma, the Hindu God of creation. At the top of the hierarchy were the Brahmins, who were mainly teachers and intellectuals and believed to have come from Brahma's head. Then came the Kshatriyas, or the warriors and rulers, supposedly from his arms. The third slot went to the Vaishyas, or the traders, who were created from his thighs. At the bottom of the heap were the Shudras, who came from Brahma's feet and did all the menial jobs. The main castes were further divided into about 3,000 castes and 25,000 sub-castes, each based on their specific occupation.
Outside of this Hindu caste system were the achhoots—the Dalits or the untouchables. (from bbc.com)
Pandita's family was part of the Shudra caste—the one at the bottom that did the menial jobs. 

➜ VIEW
∞ Video Link: Basics of Hinduism

➜ LOCATE
Find the Western Ghats. They are a mountain range that stretches 1000 miles along the coast of India. This is where Pandita was born. 
∞ Map Link: Map of India

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
An Honorable Heritage p.1-6

➜ BACKGROUND
The doctrine of karma is the backbone of the religious and social system of Hinduism. Karma says that whatever someone has—whether physical appearance, financial status, personality, health, or sorrow—is a result of his past life. One goes through the cycle of reincarnation based on his dharma in a previous life. If someone gives himself to vice and moral degeneration, he will not be destroyed or cease to exist. Rather, he will continue in the cycle of reincarnation—as long as necessary—until his soul reaches nirvana and he becomes one with the Ultimate Reality. If someone lives a life of bad dharma, he will be reborn in a lower caste or as a lower life form in the next cycle. (from Ligonier Ministries)

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
A Unique Education; Famine, Death and Doubts p.7-19

• How had the beliefs of Hinduism negatively impacted Pandita and her family?

lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: An Honorable Heritage: The Pandita Ramabai Story in Her Own Words

➜ RECAP
Briefly recap what you read last time.

➜ BACKGROUND
During the Vedic Period of northern India, the cow was a symbol of wealth and prosperity, as well as one of the animals offered in ritual religious sacrifices. But over time, possibly through the influence of Buddhism and Jainism, animal sacrifices waned, and the cow emerged as a sacred symbol to Hindus. The five products of the cow—milk, curds, butter, urine, and dung—are used in purifying and healing rituals. One popular Hindu god, Krishna, spent his early life as a cowherd, further elevating the status of the cow. In some states of India, there are bans and strict regulations concerning the slaughter of cows and the eating of beef. Many rules concerning food govern a Hindu’s life—when to eat, what to eat, and who can prepare food for whom. The preparation and consumption of food are central to a Hindu’s notion of ritual purity and, ultimately, their liberation from rebirth. Not all Hindus are vegetarians, but vegetarianism is seen as an indicator of purity. Many high-caste Hindus are vegetarians. (from International Mission Board)

➜ LOCATE
Find Kolkata (formerly Calcutta). It is in the eastern part of India.  
∞ Map Link: Map of India

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Introduction to Christianity; Calcutta; Marriage and Life in Bengal; Widowhood and Poona p.21-42

• Describe some of the inconsistencies Pandita was noticing about Hinduism and the Brahmo religion.

lessons.title: 30m Church History: Grades 7-9 - Lesson 34
lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: An Honorable Heritage: The Pandita Ramabai Story in Her Own Words

➜ RECAP
Briefly recap what you read last time.

➜ BACKGROUND
In the middle of the 19th century, a spiritual revival known as the Oxford Movement began in the Church of England. Those involved came to be known as Anglo-Catholics whose aim was to recall the Church of England to its origins and to restore reverence and beauty in worship. Out of the Oxford Movement came the first religious orders to be founded since Henry VIII of England dissolved the monasteries in 1536. The Community of St Mary the Virgin was one of the first religious orders founded at this time in 1848, in Wantage, England. A religious order consists of members who lead a contemplative lifestyle devoted to prayer and service. There were branches in India, which is where Pandita first met them. 

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
England: Being Drawn to the Religion of Christ, Finding Christ p.43-68

• What were some of the differences between Hinduism and Christianity that initially drew Pandita to accept Christianity?
• Why do you think she still felt like something was missing when she first believed the doctrines of Christianity?
• How did everything change for her when she came to know Christ personally as her Lord and Savior?

terms.content:
Church History: Grades 7-9
· Renewal: The Church that Expands Outward: Tell all you know about a person from church history that you read about this term and how their life and mission impacted others.


Bible History: Grade 9
· God's Big Picture: Choose one: 
a) What does the author mean by “God's people, God's place, and God's rule and blessing”?
b) List the Old Testament covenants and explain how God's grace is shown through them AND Tell what you know about the "new covenant"
c) Create The Kingdom of God Chart for “The pattern of the kingdom, The perished kingdom, and The promised kingdom.”

Church History: Grades 7-9
· Spurgeon: A Biography: Tell about what you have learned of Charles Spurgeon's life and ministry over this term of reading.

New Testament: Grade 9
·  Galatians: Choose one:
a) Tell how Paul addresses the believers in Galatia regarding faith and law. What are some ways the Gospel was being undermined? 
b) What is meant by “It is for freedom that Christ has set us free. Stand firm, then, and do not let yourselves be burdened again by a yoke of slavery” (Gal. 5:1). 
c) Discuss, “Since we live by the Spirit, let us keep in step with the Spirit” (Gal. 5:25).


Bible History: Grade 9
· God's Big Picture: How is "God's promise of the kingdom partially fulfilled in the history of Israel"?
OR
Discuss Figure 28 (p.110), "The End of the Old Testament" telling what you know of God's people, God's place, and God's rule and blessing as it relates to the time periods depicted.

Church History: Grades 7-9
· An Honorable Heritage: The Pandita Ramabai Story in Her Own Words: Tell all you know about Pandita's journey from being born into a Hindu family to later becoming a Christian. How did her beliefs and life change when she put her faith in Christ?

Bible History: Grade 9
· God's Big Picture: If you had to answer someone's question about what the Bible was all about, how would you do it using the framework of God's Big Picture as your model?
